speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 3. Today is January 24th, 1996. Our survivor is Rabbi Nathan Lipschitz. Could you please continue telling about your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: The answer that you're leaving the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said to the people around him-- they're very serious, because this was-- he used the like to tell, here and there, a joke. But this wasn't the time for it, so he said this, I want to have this regular clothing. You know, he always had the [INAUDIBLE] to say he didn't want to be a rabbi. He said he was afraid he's not good enough for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, I want-- on the way, if I'll wear the rabbinical clothes, I may be pushed in the side, to go in the ditches of the-- when the- because [INAUDIBLE] will come on the way. This he was sure. So I will be-- because I look like a rabbi, I'll be put on the side. That's how-- the way he thought to himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: But-- so I'll change the clothing to look like any other Jew so at least I will go together with the Jews. I wouldn't be pushed in the side. This was the way he felt then. And we started to run, walking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say besides my grandfather, my sister, my-- her husband, maybe another 30 people. |

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you want to go? Where did you think you could go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Closer to the-- to the east of-- of Galicia, which there were rumors that the Germans-- that the Russians were coming, so we were-- we didn't-- we weren't afraid of the Russians to do things that we were afraid of the Germans will do.

speaker: interviewer
text: We walked until some sort of a village. There was a young fellow with his sister. They ran away, together with us. And they had-- we didn't have any money. Who had money laying around? Not my zayde's house, because when he had money, people came and left some money, at night, he-- when he [NON-ENGLISH], everything went away to give away to poor people. He send me, the carpenter. Never there was never money in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had some money, [INAUDIBLE] a brother, sister. The name was Schulere, Fischel Schulere and his sister. And somehow we were successful and they paid for a horse and buggy. So, at least, the zayde, with the bubbeh-- the bubbeh was a very heavy-- the zayde was thinner-- and the uncle who had polio, could-- very difficult for him to walk-- went on that wagon. And then there was still enough place more, so we sort of rotate. A few people went up, then went [? down ?] to walk. And this is how we went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from town to town, village to town.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very interesting thing, to me it was, why walking on the roads? This was still walking. Oh yeah, maybe they were already on the wagon. They went slowly. You couldn't go fast, because the roads were crowded with wagons and army.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk. And all of a sudden, a-- a Polish officer, with a big, beautiful horse and a officer's uniform, he sees me. He runs over to me on the horse. And he had a little stick. And he hits me over the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, you Jew. You are going to run around here? I'm fighting. I'm fighting. You're going to walk around here? And he hit me with-- this was my first feeling of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went like this. And due to the-- to the circumstances that has only a wagon and that only a few people could go, we rotated. So I got knocked out. I didn't have any money. So-- so wherever-- our crowd, wherever we saw somebody who you knew that was running, you ask for a ride. And some gave you. Some didn't give you. Some were too crowded. And I was lucky enough one time that there were a few Jewish people from Tarnow in a truck. They were I think in food-- wholesale food business. They had a truck. So they gave me a ride in that truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I lost-- departed myself from my grandfather and the whole crowd. And I thought they were going to go farther. They had an empty truck. They did not let me. They let me off. After a few hours they let me off. So I lost myself between my grandfather and the whole crowd, my sister, the whole crowd.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they arrived next to Lemberg, to Lwow. My zayde settled down. There was some-- my-- my-- my brother-in-laws and uncle, who had the shul. There was a rabbi, there in Lemberg, so there is where my zayde made his quarters, to have a shul where to daven. And I arrived later, because I was in Lemberg, as I mentioned before, because of my brother had to come and help with kosher food to the army bases. So that's where we met again together. And, of course, the Russians were in already. But when I got departed from them, I had my experience that was pretty bad, because [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] finally, the Germans caught up with me. And this was the first day of Rosh Hashanah. I was running as a refugee to a little town by the name of Ulanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where do you go? To the rabbi's house. And there was nothing there yet. Here, and there, Polish soldiers around.

speaker: interviewee
text: Came Erev Rosh Hashanah. That was the eve of Rosh Hashanah. We went in to the rabbi's house. And some Jewish men then gave me where to sleep. Rosh Hashanah, we went in to daven. Rosh Hashanah at the rabbi's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, sure enough, there was hand-to-hand [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fights between the Polish army and the German army in that little town. We were standing at the window and seeing this-- what is it called?-- hand-to-hand-- Combat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Combat, yes. And, finally, middle of davening, there was the-- was a rabbi there, a very, very gray guy, short man and old man-- old man. He must have been at least in his 70s. And, all of a sudden, the-- the little town went hand-- from hand to hand, three, four times who controlled it, between the Polish and the German soldiers. And then, all of a sudden, it got quiet. We didn't know who is-- who is going to be-- all of a sudden, a German soldier, we see him through the window, comes up the steps and knocks on the door, very-- [INAUDIBLE] I don't know if I could go through another fear like this, if the heart would-- would-- would survive. When he knocked on that door we all thought we going to be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somehow, before the war, we didn't believe that it could be so. Somehow, there was a feeling that they will kill. Why? We didn't have any-- yes, we did hear already in other towns where they occupied and people ran, that they shot Jewish people here and there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were so-- I tell you, if I wouldn't have been a younger, 21 years old, and strong-- it's [? unbelievable. ?] To describe that fear is unbelievable. I think fear is the biggest pain that a person could endure. I think so. The next might be thirsty or then hungry. But this is the biggest fear, to my estimation. Anyway, he knocked on the door. So we thought, who knows [? what? ?] That-- that little rabbi, [LAUGHS] you know, old man, goes over, opens the door. He says in German, he goes like this, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. [LAUGHS] The German says, [SPEAKING GERMAN], the soldier. And he went away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate that? Translate what you just said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the rabbi says to the soldier, I am the oldest of the Jews in this town. I guarantee you with my life that there is no Polish hidden soldiers in my house. I guarantee you with my life. [LAUGHS] We were all astound to hear the old men, and in perfect German practically. So he says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means thank you. And he saluted and left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we all-- you know. And we were there-- this was-- by the way, this little town was supposed to be, this was-- no, not that little town. We were there until after the Rosh Hashanah, another day or two. And then, we-- we, I say, whoever was around, where are we going to run? Run away from the Germans? They're here? Where are we going to run? So we started to go back the day after Rosh Hashanah with a few people. That little trip was only about five, six kilometers-- maybe eight kilometer. I don't know exactly. It was very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the San-- there was a river, San was the name-- was between these little small towns. On the way, the Germans were filling the road with trucks and with all kinds of wagons, all kinds of equipments, and they saw us. And they were hollering and cursing and-- And then one thing, there was a guy with us-- his name was Frankel, from Limanowa-- who his things with him was like this cane. He walked with a cane type of a valise. He had all his stuff there. Somehow, this was like a thorn in the German eyes-- the German's eyes, that he probably has a machine gun there in that. There were other valises they weren't-- when they saw this, we had to stop and they had to open up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once we were stopped with him. And they opened up, and they carried on. They were tallit and teffilin. They ripped the tallit and threw the teffilin. And cursing, you dirty Churchill, you dirty-- Roosevelt they called Rosenfeld. The Jewish Rosenfeld, president. And cursed, aus Palestine, aus-- there would be no more Palestine. Cursed. And finally, then pack yourself around back. So we-- so we packed. While packing, they started to run, they shot at us. But they didn't shoot to-- to kill, just to make us more frightened. The fear there is-- you know-- German army shoots at you, you know? On one of the trucks, a soldier was there. He had a chop-- chop knife, to chop meat, you know? So he showed us, and said in German, this is what we're going to chop up all the Jews. This is-- So we went back, in other words, not continuing forward, but backwards. We're going back home to one little town. The name was Nisko. Who did you meet there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you meet up with there? Any relatives? Any friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But, in Nisko we went-- we came in-- yeah. Before that little town, I begged the few people that we were together, have a heart, the [NON-ENGLISH]. Let's rest a little bit, because I felt THAT I-- the fear that was left-- I couldn't walk on the roads. The roads were full with Germans. I had to rest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went in. We looked some houses on the road. And we saw one empty house. We were afraid of the Polish, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the back of this house. It was empty. And we stood there maybe an hour and half, two hours [INAUDIBLE], until we felt-- I especially felt that we could walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we walked into a little town, which the name was Nisko. And where you go? You don't anybody in that little town. So we went to the [INAUDIBLE]. And the [INAUDIBLE] was one candle burning, not lights, [INAUDIBLE]. We were sitting there. Maybe we'll sleep there. After a while, not too long, two-- two women came in, a girl and a married woman. She says, are there Jewish boys here? Yeah. Could you maybe come up? The men in the house went away, you know, because knowing that the Germans come in, they were afraid right away that they'll take the men and do-- so they went out to a village to the goyim, the Polack that there were friends of theirs and customers of theirs to-- until this will quiet down, the marching in. So they were alone. Well, they invited us. And they had a very big store, piece goods. Ready-made clothing was not as popular. It was very often piece goods and going to the tailor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very large store. And they had-- you know, it was rolls of merchandise, of wool and silks, everything. And they needed our helps, because they had put it all to the basement. Now they wanted-- they thought also maybe is going to be a normal life, brought it up. So we were carrying it to help them out. We slept there one night, I think, or two. And then the-- the men came back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were still with them together until, all of a sudden-- wait a minute. Yom Kippur. Yeah. That was Yom Kippur there. And they didn't bother us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The interesting thing was that one day, we were short on cigarettes. One of the girls there-- there were two single girls there-- [INAUDIBLE]-- went out to the German soldiers and asked them for-- for cigarettes. And they gave. Just like [INAUDIBLE]. You know, when it was quiet, when they didn't-- And everything was quiet. And all of a sudden, this was supposed to be-- all of a sudden they said all the Jews have to leave town, because the San, that river, was supposed to be the border between Russia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't care where you go. So we all started to go to this little-- to the next town. That was Ulanow, back to where I was Rosh Hashanah. This was the day after Yom Kippur. And I went with this family. Now, this family had a few wagons in the front of the house to take all the goods, the piece goods along, you know, to have a little-- little stack for-- for livelihood, for living. The Germans were standing there, officers. And there was a lot of Polish people, mostly fellows and girls, laughing, having fun. And the German officers, were [INAUDIBLE]-- if a nice piece came in, took it away, give it her, give it to him. They were having a good time. And, after a while, when most of the valuable stuff were given away and taken away, some of the very good stuff they put back to the store. They wanted to take it over, But some of the stuff they give to the Polish. And they gave it, put it  back. And finally, we had very little. We, I says. I was with them, but it wasn't mine. I think that the name of this family was Nestle. And we tried to-- we left the town. But I want to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you plan to go at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. To the Russian side. Russian side didn't mean Russia. Means the Polish side that was supposed to be taken over by Russia, because Russian army had marched out to take a part of Poland. There was a pact between Russia and Germany. But one little-- small little episode that has a connection with this. I had on me a ladies' golden watch and a diamond ring.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was going back from the wedding with my sister-in-law, her ring was split. The gold-- the gold, the ring gold was split. And I had to go to the big town. And she was about to go to my-- to my little town. In the little town, there was no goldsmiths. So I took it to be able to fix it for her. Before the war broke out, I had it on me. Oh. Another thing. Before that order they gave out that all Jews have to leave, there was another order that all gold, silver, and money has to be given back to the Germans. But you have so little-- so much you can have. But any gold, diamond, silver has to be given back-- given to them. Otherwise, punishable by death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not then. That was in the beginning. There was no yellow stars then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other rules do you remember, besides with the gold and silver?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll get to that. No, there wasn't at the moment any other rules. [BACKGROUND NOISE] If it's there, I don't need to answer it. Only if it's here.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Don't worry about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had on me--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. You can continue talking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You don't have to wait for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that ring, that diamond ring, and a watch. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But she can't say who it is. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Could you continue? Could you-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Keep talking, please. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- a Polish, in other words, a Volksdeutsche. It's called a Volksdeutsche. He was a German by-- by birth or by nationality, but he lived in Poland. And he was very good friends with some of the Jews there, and especially with this family. When this law came out that every gold and silver has to be given back, I was afraid. I didn't want to give this. It's my sister-in-law's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even not, so who was-- so I asked that [INAUDIBLE] was his name, like a Jewish name. I asked him, do you think that this law is for only people that live here, or I'm only a   passerby, if the law applies to me too, I asked. Whatever he answered me, I don't remember. But, of course, what I did-- because we were afraid that they're going to come and search everybody. I went in this-- in this house of this Nestles. There was like a little backyard, a little bigger than a normal backyard. Was sand there, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and took those two pieces, and I buried them. When we leave, if we leave, I'll take it back. But now, I buried it. That was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, as I told you, a day or two later, the-- we had all to run. Now-- and I didn't have a chance or was afraid to dig this up and take it with me, so it was left there. Now, going back, we went on these wagons. And we went to go over past the San, that is that river, to go into the other little town, which was called Ulanow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a lot of-- quite a number of Polish teenagers who ran after us and wanted to rob. They would grab something. We-- we chased them. But we didn't go too strict. We were still afraid. But here and there, we chased them. They were only kids, not even big teenager, 13, 15. And they were always struggling with us on the way. I chased them away. They came back. Oh. And here and there, they grabbed something on the wagons. They went. Finally, we arrived to the San. The San is the river that we are supposed to cross to be on the other side. On the other side there was no more Germans. For a while, there was no Germans, because the Russians were supposed to come, or maybe they were there already. No. No. They were not there, but they were supposed to come then. We passed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that border with the wagons. And there those kids were still carrying on until we passed on the other side. We passed now. Then the wagons start to drive, to ride. I am with them. Then two of these girls came over. They were talking between themselves. One was a married woman. The-- the husband was there. And the single girl, there were two single girls. No, one single girl, I think. And they were mumbling between themselves. I didn't hear. I didn't know what they're talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then all of a sudden, that single girl came over to me. And she says, oh, you know we have such a troubles. What is it? You see what they robbed us all of. Oh, if he could-- and we had-- and they had some real money and also some diamond hidden in that yard, buried.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, oi, we can't go back, because the-- the order was-- but you, as a traveller, as a stranger not from that town, they wouldn't do nothing. You can go in there. And she starts crying to me, not really crying. Oh, if you could--

speaker: interviewer
text: And I says, who is going to back with-- you crazy? It's dangerous. [INAUDIBLE] But to you, it isn't. I said, no, I can't. I can't do it. How am I going to go back? One of these wagons with a farmer who was the-- was their customer and they knew him well. And he's ready to go back with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says no, no, until, you know, cherchez la femme, I got softened up and she started to cry. We are poor now. We wouldn't have a piece of bread. And we will never forget [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you afraid of? To make the story short, I went back with that farmer. Now, that farmer was promised like this, that I have-- first of all, this was a stupid promise, that I will be able to go into the store and take out some material and give him for that. On top of that, I'll dig out some money. I'll give him some money and all this. And we went. I came back there with him. It was still daylight, noon or something like that. And what do I see? That a soldier is marching up and down that house on the sidewalk, watching that store, because they had put in so much money-- so much material there. It was a store with a fortune of money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: If you have-- I got stupid. And I watched him, somehow, when they did something to me, the fear was over, the [NON-ENGLISH]. Otherwise, somehow the normal-- I wasn't afraid. If there was nothing happened, I wasn't afraid. I waited. When he marched this way, I snuck into that yard. It was like a fence with boards around. I snuck into that yard, and I-- Yeah. Where they put their valuables, they told me like this. There is-- there was a hole and under the hole was boards. And on the boards was-- was sand, again. And there was some box, all the sandbox, on top of that. They move that away and they put it there, so that's where I'm going to find it. The soldier marches up and down there. And I'm inside looking for that money. What happened, what we found out later, I'll tell you how, is that the Germans had somehow moved that sandbox. And it was maybe 12 feet away from that spot, so I didn't find the real spot. My [INAUDIBLE] finding was that box with the sand. So I go down and I dig and I dig. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [INAUDIBLE] all I had on me was a pair of pants and a little sweater and a beret, no beard. The Germans had-- I had already a beard, but the Germans had cut off half, so I went and cut off the rest. How'd they cut that off? When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: They go over with the machine. Zoom, zoom, zoom. They did it to my grandfather. They-- to them it was a big holiday. They enjoyed themselves. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm laying there in the sand and digging. And all of a sudden, I see boots. I look up, I see the German boots. I look up. [SPEAKING GERMAN], the German with that rifle is standing there. [SPEAKING GERMAN], you know, in German, screaming.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I started, bubba, bubba, bubba, bubba. So I tell them that I had something here. And I said-- and I had my stuff. I had the ring with that watch I had taken out. So I tell them, I had something. And somebody told me that because I'm a traveler, not from here, that law doesn't apply to me. And therefore, I hide it. And now I came to try and get it. But why are you digging so much? I says, I had another piece. Ah, you are digging for gold or silver from the people that live here. I said, no. Anyway, he didn't believe me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Took me out. I was full with sand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, but what are you so much full of sand for? I said, well, I came to wash myself up, because there was water coming out from a pipe there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it didn't make no sense what I said. And then you don't have enough time to go over your mind, because you see death in front of you. So they take me out through the street. And then, somehow-- I don't know how it happened, if he called or somebody else. An officer came. He was like a colonel or a major. He came over here and talked to me and tried to find again, again, and again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I keep on saying-- sticking to my story. He doesn't want to believe me. He says, no. Then that [INAUDIBLE], that Volksdeutsche, came over there somehow. It was like the town came around and one was talking to another. He had a band, you know, like from the police, from the occupation army, was like watching or helping out. He was a Volksdeutsche for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says, yes, [INAUDIBLE]. He says, who knows you? And somehow, it came out that he knows me from before. And he tells the story that I asked him about if I'm a passerby, if I have to. And he told me that I have to give that and I didn't. He wanted to-- anyway, to make the story-- it took quite a while, back and forth, back and forth. We're going to stop here and we'll continue.
